If that's correct, I'd prefer not to implement this.
An explicit warning or error might be OK, but there would be a performance hit in the non-exceptional case. There is also the more general question of whether it would be preferable for discrete distributions to produce floats when integers would overflow.
@tirthasheshpatel @chrisb83 - we would appreciate your opinions here."}, {"author": "eluerken-wm", "body": "Thank you so much @tupui, @WarrenWeckesser  & @rkern!  I am sorry that I didn't reply sooner, I didn't get these notifications.  This was super informative.

I'm trying to go through the threads here, but it still seems like this issue is not fully resolved, is that fair?  I am still getting the same issues and it sounds like the proposed solution is to just flip the negative sign, is that correct?
"}, {"author": "rkern", "body": "We never actually give out geometrically distributed numbers because of the limitations of finite floating point and integer arithmetic. We always have a cutoff. As a matter of design of practical numerical distribution samplers, I'm very happy to have all of the probability mass in the excluded tail be assigned to the largest representable integer. This is the case even when the parameters are of typical size and only negligible amounts of probability mass are in that excluded tail; this case with extreme parameters isn't really exceptional. The overflow to negatives is a pure accident of (my) careless implementation and not a helpful feature in any way. We've already made the change for `Generator`; I think we should fix up the results that come from `RandomState` and older versions of `Generator` here.

> I am still getting the same issues and it sounds like the proposed solution is to just flip the negative sign, is that correct?

Not quite: make the result `np.iinfo(result.dtype).max`. That's typically one less than the negative of the negative integer that you get."}, {"author": "mdhaber", "body": "@rkern it looks like you reopened this. Are you willing to submit a fix or review one? If so, I'm willing to help find someone to do the other half so that we can resolve this. "}, {"author": "rkern", "body": "I'll certainly review."}, {"author": "mdhaber", "body": "@OmarManzoor @dschmitz89 I thought I'd bring this issue to your attention in case you'd like to submit a PR."}, {"author": "dschmitz89", "body": "From what I understood @rkern 's proposed solution is to swap out all negative values for the maximum possible integer.

Would it not be better to raise an error or at least a `RuntimeWarning`? Otherwise, the method will silently fail without users noticing it."}, {"author": "rkern", "body": "The behavior in this edge cases used to be undefined behavior in C. One some compilers and platforms, you got the maximum integer and others you got the most negative integer. We've fixed it upstream in `Generator` to uniformly do the former. The remainder to fix here is for the codepaths still using the legacy `RandomState` which will still be platform-dependent.

I'm definitely against raising an error dependent on the values that come out the end; that's just a recipe for heisenbugs that are difficult to track down. I'm mildly okay with warnings based on the _input_ parameters where the expectation of drawing from the excluded region hits some threshold. I'm not in any particular mood to define that threshold, though.

A reminder: all of the results from our finite implementations are "failures" to draw from the true distribution. Some are just more obvious than most."}, {"author": "mdhaber", "body": "> I'm mildly okay with warnings based on the input parameters where the expectation of drawing from the excluded region hits some threshold. I'm not in any particular mood to define that threshold, though.

TLDR: For `p > 1e-17`, it's very unlikely to ever get observations clipped to the maximum `int64`; for `p < 1e-20`, it's all but guaranteed. So I'd suggest warning if `p < 1e-17`, assuming that it is relatively rare for people to be sampling with `1e-20 < p < 1e-17` and get the warning unnecessarily.

```python3
import numpy as np
max_int = np.iinfo(np.int64).max
rng = np.random.default_rng()
((rng.geometric(1e-17, size=10000000) >= max_int).any(),  # False
 (rng.geometric(1e-20, size=1) >= max_int).any())  # True
```

<details>
Given the number of observations requested, the maximum integer, and a threshold probability of getting an observation from the excluded region, the algebra gives a simple expression for the corresponding threshold for the shape `p`.

```python3
x = 1000  # maximum int 
n = 100  # number of observations from geometric distribution
pn = 0.75  # probability of at least one observation > x
p1 = 1 - (1 - pn)**(1/n)
p = 1 - p1**(1/(x + 1))
# approximation when p is very small: abs(1/(x + 1) * np.log(p1))

import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng(398234982523)
any_gt_x = []
for i in range(10000):
    any_gt_x.append((rng.geometric(p, size=n) > x).any())
np.mean(any_gt_x)  # 0.7519
```

For example, if `x` is the maximum `int64`, 1 million observations are drawn, and our threshold probability is 0.1%, the threshold `p` is `2.4851459794772703e-18`. 

So if `p < 2.485e-18`, there is at least a 0.1% chance of one or more of the one million observations being clipped at the maximum integer.

But if we set `x` the maximum `int64`, the threshold for `p` is surprisingly insensitive to the number of observations (up to say, 1e15) and even the probability threshold we choosem, leading to the TLDR suggestion.

</details>

If this sounds good as a threshold for emitting a warning in NumPy, we can do this for `RandomState` in SciPy, too. In the meantime, I've opened gh-21355 to clip at the maximum integer. "}, {"author": "fancidev", "body": "I think it’s better to handle the situation based on the output rather than input. After all, for any input there is non-zero probability that the generated random number overflows, and the function still has to handle this. And warning on small input seems too eager to me: it’s like warning on allocating a huge block of memory that has 99% chance of failure and 1% chance of success; I guess the typical behavior would be to just do it and report error if it happens.

While floating point arithmetic is approximate and is usually addressed by rounding, the situation of integer overflow here is a bit different in my opinion. *Saturation is not rounding, and truncation is not approximation*. Therefore I think it’s worth doing something in addition to saturation (which I think is a reasonable choice for the integer domain).

I would personally prefer a RuntimeWarning. If it is deemed too noisy, I think it is worth at least documenting the saturation behavior. I think saturation might not seem the most obvious choice to a Python user; to me the more “Pythonic” behavior might be returning a “BigInteger” or raising a RuntimeError. (The former may be too computationally intensive, and the latter may be too intrusive, so I guess they are not very good choices for rvs.)"}, {"author": "rkern", "body": "I definitely agree that the saturation behavior should be documented. I still think it's acceptable to do nothing except document the behavior (Matt's findings concerning the parameter space is very informative to include, as well as an example of how one can check for saturation oneself).

But if we do want to have a warning, let me make one more pitch for doing it based on the passed input parameters rather than observing a saturated output. My main principle when it comes to warnings is that they need to be actionable for the user of the function. If the user can't change anything under their control to avoid triggering the warning, don't warn. As a kind of corollary, I think that when users make choices under their control that would lead to the warning-worthy outcomes depending on the things that are outside of their control, we should usually still warn them. Otherwise, we get heisenbugs that are hard to track down. A coder uses `geom.rvs()` with these extreme parameters and it happens to work fine with the PRNG state they tested it, but then in actual use, there are a bunch of warnings. But only sometimes.

There are two things that determine whether or not this `rvs()` method saturates the output: the parameters and the PRNG state. Practically speaking, the user only controls the parameters, not the PRNG state (certainly not in a way to control whether or not it saturates, except in contrived, party-trick scenarios). Every PRNG state is equally valid (or invalid), so I think it behooves us to think in terms of the ensemble of possible results when deciding to warn, not just when a particular PRNG state happens to push the result to the saturation limit. Our finite arithmetic always distorts the probability distribution, even aside from saturation. Under this rubric, we consider it warning-worthy when the distortion is high enough (for some value of "enough").  Even when we have very reasonable `p` values like 0.3, we're distorting the distribution. AFAICT, when `p==0.3`, our algorithm can _never_ generate the maximum `int64` integer (or even much smaller integers); it is _not_ actually true that every parameter choice has a finite chance of saturating. We just don't care about that particular distortion because the true probabilities were so low to begin with that the amount of distortion to the distribution is small. Working through the ziggurat constants, I think it's really only _possible_ to start saturating at around `p=1e-18`.

Also, more specific to this case, when `p<eps`, in addition to the saturation, we also start to observe wider and wider gaps between possible integer outputs below the saturation limit. That's an additional distortion that is only possible to detect by looking at the input parameters and not the result."}, {"author": "lucascolley", "body": "> My main principle when it comes to warnings is that they need to be actionable for the user of the function. If the user can't change anything under their control to avoid triggering the warning, don't warn. As a kind of corollary, I think that when users make choices under their control that would lead to the warning-worthy outcomes depending on the things that are outside of their control, we should usually still warn them. Otherwise, we get heisenbugs that are hard to track down. A coder uses geom.rvs() with these extreme parameters and it happens to work fine with the PRNG state they tested it, but then in actual use, there are a bunch of warnings. But only sometimes.

This is very convincing to me!"}, {"author": "mdhaber", "body": "What should happen when the function can detect that there may be a problem but:
a) can't be sure, and
b) can't suggest what to do (besides solving a different problem).

For example:
```python3
import numpy as np
from scipy import linalg
rng = np.random.default_rng()
n = 1000
A = np.tril(rng.random((n, n)))
b = rng.random(n)
x = linalg.solve(A, b)
# LinAlgWarning: Ill-conditioned matrix (rcond=1.55382e-21): result may not be accurate.
```

`np.linalg.solve(A, b)` silently returns garbage, and I'm not yet convinced that's better. Is the actionable thing that the warning could suggest is using an arbitrary precision library or somehow fixing their problem?

There are other similar situations in SciPy where I think it is better to at least warn if the alternatives are not warning or raising an error. (There might be other, better options than these, but sometimes it's hard to say what it would be, or hard to implement within the existing constraints.)"}, {"author": "rkern", "body": "I'm not necessarily saying that the warning message has to suggest any particular action, just that whether we issue a warning or not shouldn't hinge on something that's outside of a user's control. We often don't have nearly enough information about the user's context to suggest the right place to intervene, which might be far upstream of our library function. People solve linear systems for lots of reasons in lots of different domains, and how the ill-conditioned matrix comes into play varies. The root cause might be because the mesh in the PDE discretization is less than ideal. It might be something that could be resolved by numerical preconditioning (possibly with a different kind of solver) regardless of where the matrix comes from.

No warning message has to be comprehensive, in fact, being short and internet-searchable can be more valuable because people can find documentation and discussions that help them explore their options with higher context."}, {"author": "fancidev", "body": "@rkern Makes sense to me!

While the shape parameter has to be really small to trigger saturation (with non-negligible probability) for the geometric distribution, saturation could be more frequent for fat-tailed distributions. For example, for the Pareto distribution (whose survival probability is $x^{-\alpha}$), if $\alpha=0.001$, there is about half chance that a random number saturates to `np.inf`.

I think the saturation behavior for floating-point numbers could be used as a reference for integers. For most operations defined in IEEE-754, in addition to returning `+inf`, a (hardware) floating point exception is often raised unless it is suppressed (but it is often suppressed in practice).

There is no `inf` for integers, so the max integer is a natural choice for saturation.
Raising an error (or warning) on integer saturation might be a bit too intrusive, especially considering that floating point exceptions are often suppressed possibly for this reason. Therefore I agree that documenting the saturation behavior sounds good enough (and doing more might not be desirable).

(As for the Pareto distribution, since it is known as an extreme value distribution and is often used to model excess over a threshold, the saturation behavior is particularly appropriate for such usage.)"}, {"author": "mdhaber", "body": "The underflow issue is resolved, so closing. Follow-up requests for warnings may be opened with NumPy, and SciPy will follow suit."}]
accepted_answer_body:
"author": "tupui",
        "body": "Hi @eluerken-wm, thank you for reporting.

This is actually an error coming from NumPy. Behind the scene, `stats.geom.rvs` is making a call to the `geometric` distribution from NumPy.

```python
np.random.geometric(p=1e-20)
# -9223372036854775808
```

Now, this issue is only present with the legacy random number generator interface. And if you use the new `np.random.Generator`, the problem is fixed:

```python
np.random.default_rng().geometric(p=1e-20)
# 9223372036854775807
```

And you can verify this in SciPy by passing a proper `Generator` object, which is the recommended way:

```python
geom.rvs(1e-20, random_state=np.random.default_rng())
# 9223372036854775807
```

Hence, I am going to close this issue."

question_body:
Repository: scipy
Python version: 3.13
Requirements:
scipy==1.15.2
numpy==2.1.3

Issue description:
BUG: sparse: Potential Memory Leak in `{csr,csc}_{array,matrix}.copy`
### Describe your issue.

I think it's possible there is a memory leak with `{csr,csc}_{matrix,array}.copy` as the current memory increases monotonically in this example, but maybe I'm missing something here! I believe the issue (from a flamegraph someone else had) was coming from https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/689ebb9d0332b025d604b755febb68fb0d017a40/scipy/sparse/_compressed.py#L1338

cc @lucamarconato

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from __future__ import annotations

import sys
import tracemalloc

import numpy as np
import scipy
import scipy.sparse as sp

print(scipy.__version__, np.__version__, sys.version_info)
scipy.show_config()


def main():
    for format in ["csr", "csc"]:
        matrices = [
            sp.random_array((10_000, 1000), format="csr", rng=np.random.default_rng())
            for _ in range(100)
        ]
        tracemalloc.start()
        print(f"Starting memory for {format}", tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()[0])
        for mat in matrices:
            mat.copy()
            print(
                f"Current memory usage for {format}:",
                tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()[0],
            )
        tracemalloc.stop()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

"author": "nickodell",
        "body": ">I think it's possible there is a memory leak with `{csr,csc}_{matrix,array}.copy` as the current memory increases monotonically in this example

I'm unable to reproduce this behavior after running the example for an increased number of iterations. For me, the leak stops at around 80KB. 

Here's how I modified the example:

```
from __future__ import annotations

import sys
import tracemalloc

import numpy as np
import scipy
import scipy.sparse as sp

print(scipy.__version__, np.__version__, sys.version_info)
scipy.show_config()


def main():
    for format in ["csr", "csc"]:
        matrices = [
            sp.random_array((10_000, 1000), format=format, rng=np.random.default_rng())
            for _ in range(100)
        ]
        tracemalloc.start()
        print(f"Starting memory for {format}", tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()[0])
        for i in range(100):
            for mat in matrices:
                mat.copy()
            print(
                f"Current memory usage for {format}:",
                tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()[0],
            )
        tracemalloc.stop()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

The behavior of these two examples suggests two things to me:

1. The allocated memory is not unbounded.
2. The garbage collector could collect all but 110 bytes of this memory, if it chose to. However,  Python won't run the garbage collector until enough garbage piles up to justify the CPU cost.

question_title: BUG: scipy.stats.kstest returns NaN starting in scipy 1.12
question_body:
Repository: scipy
Python version: 3.10
Requirements:
scipy==1.13.0
numpy==1.25.2

Issue description:
BUG: scipy.stats.kstest returns NaN starting in scipy 1.12
### Describe your issue.

When the input contains both negative and positive infinity, `scipy.stats.kstest` returns `NaN`. This appears to have been introduced in scipy 1.12.0

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy

vals = [-np.inf, 0, 1, np.inf]
result = scipy.stats.kstest(vals, scipy.stats.cauchy.cdf)
print(f"scipy v{scipy.__version__}: {result}")
```

Output in scipy 1.11.4:
```
scipy v1.11.4: KstestResult(statistic=0.25, pvalue=0.90625, statistic_location=0.0, statistic_sign=-1)
```
Output in scipy 1.12.0:
```
scipy v1.12.0: KstestResult(statistic=nan, pvalue=nan, statistic_location=nan, statistic_sign=nan)
```
Output in scipy 1.13.0:
```
scipy v1.13.0: KstestResult(statistic=nan, pvalue=nan, statistic_location=nan, statistic_sign=nan)
```

comments:
[{"author": "mdhaber", "body": "As a quick fix, you can use:
```python3
stats.kstest(vals, scipy.stats.cauchy.cdf, _no_deco=True)
# KstestResult(statistic=0.25, pvalue=0.90625, statistic_location=0.0, statistic_sign=-1)
```"}, {"author": "mdhaber", "body": "I ran into the same thing recently. 

What was new in SciPy 1.12 is that `kstest` got `nan_policy` support, and that uses the old `_contains_nan` function to check whether there are NaNs. 

The `_contains_nan` function has (for a long time) detected NaNs by summing all elements of the array and checking whether the result is NaN. It looks like the rationale was that this saves memory, but when you add `-inf + inf`, a NaN is introduced. 

I'd suggest we get rid of this "summation" method and just do the obvious thing. `contains_nan = np.isnan(a).any()`. Er, rather, I would make the change in gh-20292, which adds array API support to `_contains_nan` and `stats.moment`, so it would be `contains_nan = xp.any(xp.isnan(a))`.

If I see a few thumbs up, I'll make the change in that PR.
"}, {"author": "thomasjpfan", "body": "scikit-learn has a similar `_assert_all_finite` where it does a sum check first (for speed). If the sum check fails then it does a `xp.any(xp.isnan(...))` to see if there was an actual nan.

XREF: https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/f7026e575a494b47b50557932b5c3ce0688fdc72/sklearn/utils/validation.py#L121-L126"}, {"author": "mdhaber", "body": "Thanks. I guess I'm not seeing the advantage in our case, though. For instance, as soon as the array gets into `ks_1samp`, [two arrays](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/7dcd8c59933524986923cde8e9126f5fc2e6b30b/scipy/stats/_stats_py.py#L8371) of the same size are created (and many intermediate arrays of the same size are created within the call to `cdf`). One of these is the same dtype as the original array, and the other is always `float64`. The temporary boolean array seems insignificant in comparison, so I'd think overall it would be worth the make the code a bit simpler and faster?"}, {"author": "fancidev", "body": "A possible trick is to use `xp.max` instead of `xp.sum`.

Though for readability `any(isnan(x))` is clearly best. And since premature optimization is the root of all evil, it may make sense to start with the obvious and only change to the obscure when performance becomes an issue."}, {"author": "thomasjpfan", "body": "In scikit-learn, we benchmarked our inf/nan check for 2d arrays and found that the memory pressure was much lower for valid input with no nans and infs.

For SciPy, if it is all 1D arrays, then it should be okay."}, {"author": "fancidev", "body": "In addition, `numpy.sum` performs pairwise summation, which is an overkill for detecting `nan` … It would be best if the Array API had a `count` function, so that you could do `count(x,nan)>0`."}, {"author": "lucascolley", "body": "Will we want this in the next bug fix release, or is it okay to wait for the next minor release?"}]

# KstestResult(statistic=0.25, pvalue=0.90625, statistic_location=0.0, statistic_sign=-1)
```"

question_title: BUG: genextreme.ppf differs from analytical ppf
question_body:
Repository: scipy
Python version: 3.9
Requirements:
scipy==1.7.1
numpy==1.21.2

Issue description:
BUG: genextreme.ppf differs from analytical ppf
### Describe your issue.

Hi,

It seems that `scipy.stats.genextreme.ppf` produce an incorrect result compared with the analytical one. Please consider the code example below.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np

from scipy.stats import genextreme


d = genextreme.rvs(0.1, 0, 1, 10000, random_state=42)
c, loc, scale = genextreme.fit(d)
numerical_ppf = genextreme.ppf(0.005, c, loc, scale)
analytical_ppf = loc + scale / c * ((-np.log(0.005))**(-c) - 1)
print(numerical_ppf, analytical_ppf)
```


### Error message

```shell
No error message
```


### SciPy/NumPy/Python version information

1.7.1 1.21.2 sys.version_info(major=3, minor=9, micro=7, releaselevel='final', serial=0)

Buggy code:

from scipy.stats import genextreme

d = genextreme.rvs(0.1, 0, 1, 10000, random_state=42)

numerical_ppf = genextreme.ppf(0.005, c, loc, scale)
analytical_ppf = loc + scale / c * ((-np.log(0.005)) ** (-c) - 1)
print(numerical_ppf, analytical_ppf)

My guess is that your analytical_ppf uses a different parameterization than scipy uses.
Check that the inverse function of your analytical_ppf actually corresponds to the scipy version. My guess is that it doesn't.

question_title: BUG: Wrong attributes when multiplying sparse array with dense vector
question_body:
Repository: scipy
Python version: 3.9
Requirements:
scipy==1.10.1
numpy==1.25.0

Issue description:
BUG: Wrong attributes when multiplying sparse array with dense vector
### Describe your issue.

Hi,

I found an un-expected behavior when elementwise multiplying a sparse array or matrix with a dense vector which has some zero elements. When using the .row & .col attributes on the result of the multiplication we get the nonzero indices of the array prior to the multiplication. However, when using the .nonzero() method on the result of the multiplication we get the correct indices for the nonzero elements. 

This issue doesn't seem to occur when the dense vector has no zero elements. 
See toy example. 

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np 
import scipy.sparse as ssr

v = np.array([0,1,0,1])
m = np.array([[0,5,0,0], [5,0,0,3], [0,0,0,0]])
m_sparse = ssr.csr_array(m)

# Multiply (elementwise) each row of m with v. 
# In scipy notation terms, the nonzero indices of resulting multiplication should be: ( [0,1], [1,3] ). 
# When using the nonzero method to extract the nonzero positions we get the nonzero indices of m_sparse (i.e. before multiplying by v).

# With .multiply() (The issue doen't occur if v does not have any zeros):
(m_sparse.multiply(v)).row # output: [0,1,1]
(m_sparse.multiply(v)).col # output: [1,0,3]
(m_sparse.multiply(v)).nonzero() # output: ([0,1], [1,3])

# A different approach that doesn't cause a problem:
(m_sparse @ ssr.diags(v, offsets=0, format='csr')).tocoo().row # output: [0,1]
(m_sparse @ ssr.diags(v, offsets=0, format='csr')).tocoo().col # output: [1,3]
(m_sparse @ ssr.diags(v, offsets=0, format='csr')).nonzero() # output: ([0,1], [1,3])
```

The Issue shows `csr_array` to create `m_sparse`, but the code that "doesn't cause a problem" seems to use `csr_matrix`. (I guess it is possible that it uses `@` instead of `*`... but `*` with `csr_array` creates the `ValueError` reported by @SebastianMunozMora )

Can you verify this @nirryde ?

**Further investigation** (assuming using `csr_matrix` to cause the error with `row` and `col`):
`m_sparse.multiply(v)` results in a `coo_matrix` with some of its "nonzero" values being `0`.
Look at `m_sparse.multiply(v).data` -> `array([5, 0, 3])`.

To remove those zero values, you need the extra step of eliminating the zeros. In your case:
`m_sparse.multiply(v).eliminate_zeros()`

Note that this means `m_sparse.row` shows the rows for the **stored values**, not for the **nonzero values**.  Perhaps this could be improved in the docs.

The choice not to remove zero values when doing element-wise multiplication is to avoid the extra computation. Note that any manipulations of the matrix are still numerically correct when zero values are stored in the matrix. The question is whether it is worth the time to remove them.  "}, {"author": "nirryde", "body": "@dschult My bad, the * in the code that works was supposed to be a @ (I edited the post).
If I understand correctly the .row and .col properties should return the nonzero indices of the sparse COO array i.e they should coincide with the output of the .nonzero() method.  Otherwise this seems inconsistent with the .row and .col properties representing the 'ij' part in the 'ijv' triplet.  


 "}, {"author": "dschult", "body": "The .row and .col properties represent the indices of the sparse COO array, but if the COO array stores some zeros, those values get rows and columns too. The attributes .row and .col only match .nonzero() **only if there aren't any zeros stored explicitly in the coo_array**.

```python
import scipy as sp;import numpy as np
A = np.arange(6).reshape(2,3)
Asp = sp.sparse.csr_array(A)
print(Asp.data)  # -> array([1 2 3 4 5])
# Add a zero value to the array
Asp[1, 0] = 0
print(Asp.data)  # -> array([1 2 0 4 5])

# switch to coo format
Acoo=Asp.tocoo(copy=False)
print("row: ", Acoo.row)     # -> array([0 0 1 1 1])
print("col: ", Acoo.col)     # -> array([1 2 0 1 2])
print("data:", Acoo.data)    # -> array([1 2 0 4 5])
print("nonzero: ", Acoo.nonzero())  # -> (array([0, 0, 1, 1], dtype=int32), array([1, 2, 1, 2], dtype=int32))

# eliminate zeros   This produces what you were expecting
Acoo.eliminate_zeros()
print("row: ", Acoo.row)     # -> array([0 0 1 1])
print("col: ", Acoo.col)     # -> array([1 2 1 2])
print("data:", Acoo.data)    # -> array([1 2 4 5])
```

This doesn't violate the idea that row and col represent the 'ij' in the 'ijv' triplet precisely when the 'v' in 'ijv' is zero."}, {"author": "nirryde", "body": "@dschult I see... I feel like the documentation does not clarify the fact that an operation (like the multiplication I did) can end up with a sparse matrix with zero elements. In other words, I expect that computations which return a sparse array will never have zero elements. Are there any use cases where we would want to explicitly store zero values?"}, {"author": "dschult", "body": "When you do a multiplication like you did, then any elements that multiply a default value (0) in the other array will result in the value zero.  It can be expensive to update all those zeros if all you want to do with that result is e.g. sum the columns. Rather than copying or reallocating the behind-the-scenes arrays in the sparse matrix, you can just store a value that is zero. It won't affect the sum's result and it will be faster and avoid another allocation of memory to hold intermediate results. 

If you do want to eliminate any zero entries explicitly stored, the `eliminate_zeros()` method is available and you should use it before any operations that would be expensive or confusing if they report locations where the value is zero. The main difficulty as I see it is when people don't know that the values can be zero -- that you should use `nonzero()` if you want to know that. I think documentation is the place where that can be fixed.

I suppose you could eliminate zeros after every operation, but as you might expect, that gets cumbersome in the code -- and would also slow down your computation of most complicated expressions. It really is better this way most of the time."}, {"author": "dschult", "body": "If this hasn't been addressed sufficiently, or if there are more comments/questions add them here and we will reopen this issue. If has been quiet for a while so I'm closing."}]

"author": "dschult",
        "body": "The .row and .col properties represent the indices of the sparse COO array, but if the COO array stores some zeros, those values get rows and columns too. The attributes .row and .col only match .nonzero() **only if there aren't any zeros stored explicitly in the coo_array**.

```python
import scipy as sp;import numpy as np
A = np.arange(6).reshape(2,3)
Asp = sp.sparse.csr_array(A)
print(Asp.data)  # -> array([1 2 3 4 5])
# Add a zero value to the array
Asp[1, 0] = 0
print(Asp.data)  # -> array([1 2 0 4 5])

# switch to coo format
Acoo=Asp.tocoo(copy=False)
print("row: ", Acoo.row)     # -> array([0 0 1 1 1])
print("col: ", Acoo.col)     # -> array([1 2 0 1 2])
print("data:", Acoo.data)    # -> array([1 2 0 4 5])
print("nonzero: ", Acoo.nonzero())  # -> (array([0, 0, 1, 1], dtype=int32), array([1, 2, 1, 2], dtype=int32))

# eliminate zeros   This produces what you were expecting
Acoo.eliminate_zeros()
print("row: ", Acoo.row)     # -> array([0 0 1 1])
print("col: ", Acoo.col)     # -> array([1 2 1 2])
print("data:", Acoo.data)    # -> array([1 2 4 5])
```

This doesn't violate the idea that row and col represent the 'ij' in the 'ijv' triplet precisely when the 'v' in 'ijv' is zero."
